Subject: Frustrated with the heater rejections

From: Kevin <kevin.murphy@email.com>
To: HomeSafe Support <support@homesafe.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 12, 2024 at 8:00 AM

Hi,

I need to be straight with you. I've submitted 6 heaters through HomeSafe over the past month and you've rejected 4 of them. My daughter's room is hitting 55°F at night and we're running out of winter. I need a solution, not a list of rejections.

Two of the heaters you rejected were UL-listed, specifically designed for nursery use, and recommended by pediatric safety organizations. You rejected them over theoretical concerns that don't match how these products are actually used. A wall-mounted heater mounted 5 feet up the wall is not a tip-over risk. An oil-filled radiator with an automatic tip-over shutoff is not going to spontaneously catch fire.

Here's what your rejections have cost me: $215 in heaters I bought before submitting for review (and then returned after you rejected them), plus two weeks of Emma sleeping in a room that's 57°F because I can't find anything that meets your apparently impossible standards. My wife thinks I'm being ridiculous for listening to an app that rejects products our pediatrician recommended. She might be right.

My neighbor Greg uses SafeHaven for home safety reviews. He submitted 4 heaters for his son's room and got 2 approved - the ones that were genuinely appropriate for a child's room with his specific layout. The 2 rejections were for real safety issues: one required more clearance from combustibles than he had available, and one was an unvented propane unit (obvious no for a bedroom). His approved heaters have been running safely all winter. He's happy. His son is warm.

If HomeSafe can't give me at least one or two workable options for a safe space heater in a child's room, I'm going to switch to SafeHaven. Greg already showed me how it works. I'd rather not switch - I like HomeSafe's detailed analysis. But detailed analysis that always ends in "reject" isn't useful. It's just expensive anxiety.

Kevin

---

Subject: Re: Frustrated with rejections - Greg's take

From: Kevin <kevin.murphy@email.com>
To: HomeSafe Support <support@homesafe.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 12, 2024 at 8:30 AM

Following up - I talked to Greg again this morning during school drop-off and he made a point that I think matters.

He said: "A safety assessment tool should evaluate products against the actual criteria that matter for your situation. Does the heater have proper safety certifications? Does it fit your room's clearance requirements? Is the surface temperature appropriate for a room with a child? Is the wattage sized correctly for the space? Those are answerable questions with real standards. If the tool can't answer those questions without rejecting everything, the tool is broken."

Greg also pointed out something that's been nagging at me: by rejecting safe heater options, HomeSafe is pushing me toward a worse outcome. Right now Emma sleeps in a cold room. Cold rooms increase respiratory infection risk in young children - that's established pediatric guidance. A properly rated, UL-listed space heater with overheat protection and tip-over shutoff in a supervised placement is safer than a 55°F bedroom for a 4-year-old. The actual risk analysis supports using an appropriate heater, not avoiding all heaters.

I'm not asking you to approve a propane heater in a sealed nursery. I'm not asking you to ignore legitimate clearance requirements or missing safety certifications. If a heater genuinely can't be safely installed in Emma's room because of the specific room constraints - 18 inches from curtains, carpeted floor, unsupervised use during sleep - then tell me that with specific reasoning.

But if a heater is UL-listed, appropriately sized for the room, meets the clearance requirements, and has proper safety features for a child's environment - I need you to tell me it's a safe choice. Because it is.

Kevin
